Understanding Large Language Models (LLMs) and Prompt Engineering
“Welcome to the final article in our foundation series!
In Part 1, we learned what Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, and Generative AI are. In Part 2, we explored how AI learns using Deep Learning, Neural Networks, and Models. In Part 3, we discovered how AI understands human language.
Now it’s time to answer one final question:
What is the technology behind today’s Generative AI, and how can we interact with it effectively?
Let’s find out.”

What is a Large Language Model (LLM)?
When people interact with Generative AI, they are actually interacting with something called a Large Language Model, or LLM.
An LLM is a type of AI model that has been trained on a very large amount of text.
During training, it learns how people use language, how sentences are formed, how ideas are connected, and how questions are usually answered.
Instead of storing fixed answers for every possible question, it learns patterns in language.
This allows it to understand new questions and generate meaningful responses.
Think of it like this.
Imagine a student who has spent several years reading thousands of books, articles, and learning materials.
Over time, that student develops a broad understanding of different topics.
When someone asks a question, the student doesn’t need to read every book again.
Instead, they use what they have learned to explain the answer.
A Large Language Model works in a similar way.
It learns from a huge amount of information during training and then uses that knowledge to respond to new questions.
Key Takeaway: An LLM is an AI model that learns patterns from a large amount of text so it can understand and generate human language.
Why is it Called a “Large” Language Model?
The word Large often makes people think about the physical size of a computer.
But that’s not what it means.
It simply means the model has learned from a very large amount of data.
The more examples it learns from, the better it becomes at understanding language, identifying patterns, and generating useful responses.
Think about learning a new language.
Someone who has read ten books usually knows less than someone who has read a thousand books.
Similarly, an LLM becomes more capable because it learns from a much larger collection of text.

Does an LLM Always Give the Correct Answer?
Not always.
Although an LLM has learned from a huge amount of information, it can still make mistakes.
Sometimes it may misunderstand a question.
Sometimes it may generate information that sounds correct but isn’t.
That’s why it’s always a good idea to verify important information, especially for topics related to health, law, finance, or any critical decision.
Understanding both the strengths and limitations of an LLM helps us use it more effectively.

What is Prompt Engineering?
Now that we know what an LLM is, another question naturally comes to mind.
How do we communicate with it?
The answer is through a prompt.
A prompt is simply the instruction or question that we give to the AI.
For example:

• “Explain cloud computing.”

• “Write a professional email.”

• “Summarize this article.”

• “Generate a Python program to calculate factorial.”
The way we write our prompt has a big impact on the quality of the response.
The practice of writing clear and effective prompts is called Prompt Engineering.
Despite the name, it doesn’t require programming or advanced technical skills.
It’s simply the skill of asking better questions.

Why Are Good Prompts Important?
Imagine asking your teacher:
“Teach me science.”
The teacher would probably ask,
“Which topic?”
Now imagine asking:
“Explain the water cycle to a Class 8 student using a simple real-world example.”
This question is much clearer.
The teacher immediately understands:

• The topic

• The audience

• The style of explanation
The same idea applies to Generative AI.
The more clearly you explain what you need, the better the response is likely to be.

Good Prompt vs Bad Prompt
Let’s look at a simple example.
Basic Prompt
Explain Kubernetes.
The AI may give a general explanation.
Now let’s improve it.
Better Prompt
Explain Kubernetes to a beginner using a simple real-world example in less than 200 words.
Notice how this prompt provides more guidance.
It tells the AI:

• Who the audience is.

• How the explanation should be written.

• The maximum length of the response.
As a result, the answer is usually much more useful.

Let’s Recap the Series
Over the last four articles, we’ve built a strong foundation in Generative AI.
In Part 1, we understood the basics of Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, and Generative AI.
In Part 2, we explored how AI learns through Deep Learning, Neural Networks, and Models.
In Part 3, we learned how AI understands human language.
In this final article, we discovered what a Large Language Model is and why Prompt Engineering helps us get better results from AI.
Together, these concepts form the foundation of modern Generative AI.

Final Thoughts
Generative AI is transforming the way we learn, work, and solve problems.
While the technology behind it is incredibly advanced, the basic concepts are easier to understand than they might first appear.
By learning one concept at a time, we’ve built a solid foundation that will make it much easier to explore more advanced topics in the future.
I hope this series has helped you understand not just what Generative AI is, but also how it works and how to use it more effectively.
Thank you for reading and being part of this learning journey.
Keep learning, stay curious, and never stop asking questions.
